title: Half-reaction
definition: A **half-reaction** shows one half of a redox process with its **electrons written explicitly**: the **oxidation** half loses them ($\mathrm{Zn} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn^{2+}} + 2\,e^-$, at the anode), the **reduction** half gains them ($\mathrm{Cu^{2+}} + 2\,e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$, at the cathode). Each half is balanced for **both atoms and charge** (the electrons carry the charge). To combine them into the overall reaction you scale each half so the **electrons cancel** — the number transferred is $n = \operatorname{lcm}$ of the two electron counts (see [[electron-ledger]]). Splitting a reaction this way is what lets you read a cell's voltage off a table of standard potentials, one half at a time (see [[standard-reduction-potential]]).
